blocks.text: Here's something most tennis players do before every session: they jog one lap, stretch their legs, hold it for a bit, then pick up their racket.
It feels like warming up. But it's not really helping them. In fact, the stretching part might actually be making them slower.
Here's what actually works — and why.
Why warming up matters
When muscles are cold, they can't fire fast. They're sluggish. The first step is late. The serve feels stiff. Reactions are slow.

When you warm up properly, muscle temperature goes up — and that literally makes muscles move faster. For every degree muscles warm up, explosive power goes up by about 3–4%. Faster serves, quicker feet, sharper reactions.

But here's the catch: the effect doesn't last long. If players warm up and then stand around for an hour, they're basically cold again. Time the warm-up so it ends close to when the session starts — right before, not 30 minutes before.
The one thing to cut

Static stretching before training. Those long holds where players bend down and count to 30 — research is very clear on this: it slows them down. It makes muscles too loose, too floppy, and that actually hurts sprint speed and serve power.
Dynamic stretching — moving through range instead of holding it — gives the same flexibility benefit without the speed cost. Cut the held stretches. Move instead.
The 4-step structure

blocks.label: Step 1 — Raise (3–5 min)
blocks.text: Get the blood moving and the muscles warm. Low-effort movement — players are not working hard yet, just waking up. Jog easy, side shuffles, high knees, butt kicks. Think of it like warming up a car engine — you don't floor it straight away.

blocks.label: Step 2 — Activate & Mobilise (7–10 min)
blocks.text: Move the joints through the ranges tennis actually needs. Leg swings, hip circles, walking lunges with a twist, arm swings, trunk rotations. No holding — keep it flowing. Research on tennis players showed a dynamic warm-up like this made serve speed 4% faster and sprint times 4% quicker compared to a static warm-up.

blocks.label: Step 3 — Potentiate (10 seconds)
blocks.text: Optional, but worth building in before serving. Right before players serve — not 10 minutes before, right before — they press their hitting arm hard against a fence post or wall as if trying to swing it forward. Hold 5 seconds, twice. In a study on real tennis players, serve speed went up 4.6 km/h just from this. No equipment, no extra time.

blocks.label: Step 4 — Start immediately
blocks.text: The warm-up keeps players ready for about 30 minutes. After 60, it's mostly gone. Don't let them stand around after it. If there's a long break mid-session, run a quick 2-minute re-raise before going hard again.
At a glance

items: Raise — easy jogging, shuffles, high knees (3–5 min)
Activate & Mobilise — leg swings, arm swings, walking lunges, trunk rotations (7–10 min)
Potentiate — 2 × 5-second arm press against wall (10 seconds, right before serving)

blocks.text: The rule

blocks.label: Rule
blocks.text: Move, don't hold. Everything in the warm-up should be moving. If players are standing still counting seconds, it's wrong before a session. Static stretches are for after training — when they're warm, the session is done, and you want to cool down and loosen up. Before training: move. After training: hold.
The routine

This follows the Raise + Activate & Mobilise sequence above as a structured group warm-up. Brief your players while they move — between the raise and the mobility work is a natural time to cue what's coming in the session. No equipment needed.
